text.title: Success Metrics Definition Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Success Metrics Definition application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: The spec MUST name exactly one North Star Metric. Two or more North Stars are rejected.
rule.rationale: Two North Stars = no North Star; the team cannot trade off between them under pressure.

rule.statement: The 3-5 Primary KPIs MUST collectively cover at least 3 of the AARRR stages. Single-stage KPI sets are rejected.
rule.rationale: Single-stage coverage hides the funnel; a Retention-only KPI set ignores the leak in Activation.

rule.statement: Every metric MUST carry an exact measurement definition: event name, filter rules, time window, exclusions. "Active users" without definition is rejected.
rule.rationale: Without exact definitions, teams argue about numbers; with definitions, they argue about strategy.

rule.statement: Every metric MUST carry a current baseline value AND a target value with a deadline. Targetless metrics are rejected.
rule.rationale: A metric without a target is a thermometer, not a decision tool.

rule.statement: Every metric MUST be tagged vanity (look-good, not decision-driving) | actionable (decision-driving). Vanity metrics MUST be flagged on dashboards.
rule.rationale: Vanity metrics quietly drive feature choices; explicit tagging exposes the influence.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
